intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to intimate, analog-feeling interfaces that feel like a trusted friend's kitchen rather than a sterile database
facade.label: Cozy kitchen counter
facade.acceptImplies: Warm, textured, personal, maybe hand-drawn elements, close-up perspective, soft lighting cues, conversational tone
facade.rejectImplies: Sleek, minimalist, corporate, distant, digital-first aesthetics, expansive layouts, impersonal efficiency

evidence.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to intimate, analog-feeling interfaces that feel like a trusted friend's kitchen rather than a sterile database
evidence.implication: Warm, textured, personal, maybe hand-drawn elements, close-up perspective, soft lighting cues, conversational tone

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer clean, uncluttered design that reduces cognitive load and decision paralysis
facade.label: Bright, minimal interface
facade.acceptImplies: User favors high-contrast, spacious layouts with simplified visual hierarchy — likely appreciates quick scanning and reduced visual noise in the app
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer richer visual detail, warmer color palettes, or more guided/structured layouts — or finds brightness/minimalism cold and impersonal

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer a single metric (prep time, difficulty score, or ingredient count) over narrative recipe descriptions
facade.label: Recipe in one number
facade.acceptImplies: Users want rapid, scannable recipe assessment — show ingredients count or complexity score before full recipe. Consider card-based layout with dominant single metric.
facade.rejectImplies: Users need more context than one number — require ingredient list, method overview, or time breakdown visible at glance. Narrative structure matters more than data density.

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer gradual reveal of recipe steps over instant display, reducing cognitive load and creating moments of discovery
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and behavior — animated introduction vs static presentation
facade.label: Ingredients fade in
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use entrance animations for recipe components, ingredient lists, and step-by-step reveals. This signals a preference for paced, guided experiences that feel less overwhelming for cooking-averse users.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer immediate, static lists. The app should show all ingredients at once without animation. This suggests cooking-haters want efficiency and clarity over aesthetic transitions.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to irreverent, self-aware humor than earnest encouragement
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — playful vs serious
facade.label: Sarcastic tone
facade.acceptImplies: The app should lean into dark humor, admit that cooking is annoying, use jokes about shortcuts and frozen food. Voice is witty and a bit cynical.
facade.rejectImplies: The app should avoid sarcasm and snark. Users prefer straightforward, encouraging, or neutral tone—no winking at the camera about hating to cook.

facade.hypothesis: For a recipe app targeting people who hate cooking, horizontal tab navigation (ingredients, steps, timer, notes) feels less overwhelming than a sidebar because users stay focused on one section at a time without spatial complexity.
facade.label: Tabs across top
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers linear, sequential task flow. Likely to accept: hidden complexity, card-based steps, collapsible sections. Suggests audience values simplicity and reduced cognitive load.
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer persistent context (sidebar shows all options always) or believe tabs waste screen space. Suggests preference for overview-at-a-glance or denser layouts.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants a calm, low-pressure environment vs. gamified, achievement-driven experience with rewards
Whether user prioritizes time estimates and difficulty ratings vs. nutritional data and ingredient sourcing details
Whether user wants instant recipe access vs. animated transitions and progressive reveals that slow down the experience

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants a calm, low-pressure environment vs. gamified, achievement-driven experience with rewards
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes time estimates and difficulty ratings vs. nutritional data and ingredient sourcing details
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant recipe access vs. animated transitions and progressive reveals that slow down the experience
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking may prefer gamified, progress-driven interfaces that break recipes into tiny wins rather than overwhelming ingredient lists, reducing cognitive load and building momentum
facade.label: Bright checklist
facade.acceptImplies: Design with checkboxes, progress bars, small victories, energetic visual feedback (colors, animations), dopamine-hit pacing—makes cooking feel like completing tasks rather than following instructions
facade.rejectImplies: Users find gamification patronizing or distracting; they want stripped-down, minimal interfaces that get out of the way; focus should shift toward ambient calm and trust rather than motivation engineering

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for Cooking Haters
draft.summary: A recipe app designed for people who want delicious meals with minimal effort. Features simple 15-minute recipes, ingredient shortcuts, and a "skip the fuss" approach to cooking.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals in 15 minutes. No fuss, no stress.

Today's Quick Picks

Garlic Butter Pasta

⏱ 12 min🔥 Easy

Boxed pasta + jarred garlic + butter. That's it. Tastes like you tried.

No ChoppingPantry

Sheet Pan Chicken & Veggies

⏱ 20 min🔥 Easy

Throw frozen chicken and pre-cut veggies on a pan. Roast. Done.

Minimal PrepOne Pan
Why QuickBite?
  • No Skill Required – Recipes assume you can boil water.
  • Shortcuts Built In – Pre-cut, frozen, jarred. All welcome.
  • Real Food – Not takeout. Actually tasty.
